title: The Old Man Who Feeds Pigeons
text_zh: 广场上有一个喂鸽子的老人。他每天上午十点准时出现，坐在同一条长椅上，从口袋里掏出一个塑料袋，里面装着碎面包。鸽子们好像认识他，他还没坐下就已经聚过来了。他一小把一小把地撒，动作很慢，像是在做一件需要耐心的精细工作。我路过了很多次，从来没见他跟任何人说话。有一次下雨，他撑着伞照样来了，鸽子也照样来了。我突然想到，也许对他来说，这不是在喂鸽子——这是他一天里最重要的约会。他和鸽子之间有一个安静的协议：你来，我就在。这种不需要语言的陪伴，也许是最不容易辜负的一种。
text_pinyin: Guǎngchǎng shàng yǒu yí gè wèi gēzi de lǎorén. Tā měi tiān shàngwǔ shí diǎn zhǔnshí chūxiàn, zuò zài tóng yì tiáo chángyǐ shàng, cóng kǒudài lǐ tāo chū yí gè sùliào dài, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe suì miànbāo. Gēzi men hǎoxiàng rènshi tā, tā hái méi zuò xià jiù yǐjīng jù guòlái le. Tā yì xiǎo bǎ yì xiǎo bǎ de sā, dòngzuò hěn màn, xiàng shì zài zuò yí jiàn xūyào nàixīn de jīngxì gōngzuò. Wǒ lùguò le hěn duō cì, cónglái méi jiàn tā gēn rènhé rén shuōhuà. Yǒu yí cì xià yǔ, tā chēng zhe sǎn zhàoyàng lái le, gēzi yě zhàoyàng lái le. Wǒ tūrán xiǎng dào, yěxǔ duì tā lái shuō, zhè bú shì zài wèi gēzi — zhè shì tā yì tiān lǐ zuì zhòngyào de yuēhuì. Tā hé gēzi zhī jiān yǒu yí gè ānjìng de xiéyì: nǐ lái, wǒ jiù zài. Zhè zhǒng bù xūyào yǔyán de péibàn, yěxǔ shì zuì bù róngyì gūfù de yì zhǒng.
text_en: There's an old man who feeds pigeons in the square. He appears at exactly ten every morning, sits on the same bench, takes a plastic bag from his pocket filled with bread crumbs. The pigeons seem to know him — they gather before he even sits down. He scatters the crumbs in small handfuls, very slowly, as if performing delicate work that requires patience. I've passed by many times and never seen him talk to anyone. Once it rained and he came anyway with an umbrella; the pigeons came anyway too. It struck me that maybe for him, this isn't feeding pigeons — it's the most important appointment of his day. Between him and the pigeons there's a quiet agreement: you come, I'll be here. This kind of companionship that needs no language may be the kind least likely to disappoint.

questions.q_zh: 下雨的时候老人怎么做？
questions.q_en: What does the old man do when it rains?

questions.q_zh: 作者认为喂鸽子对老人意味着什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think feeding pigeons means to the old man?

options.pinyin: yì zhǒng xiāomó shíjiān de fāngshì
yì tiān lǐ zuì zhòngyào de yuēhuì
options.text_en: The most important appointment of his day

options.pinyin: yì zhǒng shěng qián de fāngshì

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得这种陪伴有什么特别？
questions.q_en: What does the author find special about this companionship?

options.text: 不需要语言，最不容易辜负
options.pinyin: bù xūyào yǔyán, zuì bù róngyì gūfù
options.text_en: Needs no language and is least likely to disappoint

title: The Afternoon Nap Factory
text_zh: 中午一点到两点之间，我们整层办公室会变成一个午睡工厂。有人趴在桌上，有人歪在椅子里，有人戴着颈枕靠在墙上。打呼噜的声音此起彼伏，形成一种奇怪的交响乐。最神奇的是，所有人都在一点四十五分左右醒来——不是因为闹钟，而是因为某种集体的生物钟。醒来以后有大约五分钟的过渡期：揉眼睛、喝水、看手机、缓慢地从梦里回到现实。在那五分钟里，每个人的脸上都有一种茫然的柔软，好像壳还没有重新长回来。那是这个办公室一天中最真实的五分钟。
text_pinyin: Zhōngwǔ yì diǎn dào liǎng diǎn zhī jiān, wǒmen zhěng céng bàngōngshì huì biàn chéng yí gè wǔshuì gōngchǎng. Yǒu rén pā zài zhuō shàng, yǒu rén wāi zài yǐzi lǐ, yǒu rén dài zhe jǐng zhěn kào zài qiáng shàng. Dǎ hūlu de shēngyīn cǐqǐbǐfú, xíngchéng yì zhǒng qíguài de jiāoxiǎngyuè. Zuì shénqí de shì, suǒyǒu rén dōu zài yì diǎn sìshíwǔ fēn zuǒyòu xǐng lái — bú shì yīnwèi nàozhōng, ér shì yīnwèi mǒu zhǒng jítǐ de shēngwù zhōng. Xǐng lái yǐhòu yǒu dàyuē wǔ fēnzhōng de guòdù qī: róu yǎnjing, hē shuǐ, kàn shǒujī, huǎnmàn de cóng mèng lǐ huí dào xiànshí. Zài nà wǔ fēnzhōng lǐ, měi gè rén de liǎn shàng dōu yǒu yì zhǒng mángrán de róuruǎn, hǎoxiàng ké hái méiyǒu chóngxīn zhǎng huílái. Nà shì zhège bàngōngshì yì tiān zhōng zuì zhēnshí de wǔ fēnzhōng.
text_en: Between one and two in the afternoon, our entire floor becomes a nap factory. Some people slump over their desks, some lean sideways in their chairs, some wear neck pillows against the wall. Snoring rises and falls in waves, forming a strange symphony. The most magical part is that everyone wakes up around 1:45 — not from alarms, but from some collective biological clock. After waking there's about a five-minute transition: rubbing eyes, drinking water, checking phones, slowly returning from dream to reality. During those five minutes, everyone's face has a dazed softness, as if their shell hasn't grown back yet. Those are the most genuine five minutes of the office day.

questions.q_zh: 大家大约什么时候醒来？
questions.q_en: When does everyone wake up approximately?

questions.q_zh: 作者说醒来后五分钟有什么特别？
questions.q_en: What does the author say is special about the five minutes after waking?

options.text: 每个人脸上有一种茫然的柔软
options.pinyin: měi gè rén liǎn shàng yǒu yì zhǒng mángrán de róuruǎn
options.text_en: Everyone's face has a dazed softness

questions.q_zh: 「壳还没有重新长回来」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does 'the shell hasn't grown back yet' mean?

options.text: 人们还没回到工作状态的伪装
options.pinyin: rénmen hái méi huí dào gōngzuò zhuàngtài de wěizhuāng
options.text_en: People haven't returned to their work-mode facades

title: The Sound of Someone Cooking Downstairs
text_zh: 每天傍晚六点左右，楼下会传来炒菜的声音。先是油热的噼里啪啦声，然后是食材下锅的刺啦声，接着是锅铲翻动的节奏。如果仔细听，还能闻到油烟味从窗户飘上来。我不知道是哪一户在做饭，但我对他们的菜单比对自己邻居的名字更熟悉——从声音可以判断今天是炒菜还是炖汤，是简单的还是复杂的。周末的声音通常更丰富，持续时间更长，好像在做一顿认真的饭。平时则很快，十五分钟解决。有时候下班回来很累，什么都不想做的时候，听到楼下开始炒菜，就觉得这一天还是有着落的。不是我的饭，但那个声音本身就是一种安慰。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn zuǒyòu, lóu xià huì chuán lái chǎo cài de shēngyīn. Xiān shì yóu rè de pīlipālā shēng, ránhòu shì shícái xià guō de cìlā shēng, jiēzhe shì guōchǎn fāndòng de jiézòu. Rúguǒ zǐxì tīng, hái néng wén dào yóuyān wèi cóng chuānghù piāo shànglái. Wǒ bù zhīdào shì nǎ yí hù zài zuòfàn, dàn wǒ duì tāmen de càidān bǐ duì zìjǐ línjū de míngzi gèng shúxī — cóng shēngyīn kěyǐ pànduàn jīntiān shì chǎocài háishi dùn tāng, shì jiǎndān de háishi fùzá de. Zhōumò de shēngyīn tōngcháng gèng fēngfù, chíxù shíjiān gèng cháng, hǎoxiàng zài zuò yí dùn rènzhēn de fàn. Píngshí zé hěn kuài, shíwǔ fēnzhōng jiějué. Yǒu shíhou xiàbān huílái hěn lèi, shénme dōu bù xiǎng zuò de shíhou, tīng dào lóu xià kāishǐ chǎo cài, jiù juéde zhè yì tiān háishì yǒu zhe luò de. Bú shì wǒ de fàn, dàn nàge shēngyīn běnshēn jiù shì yì zhǒng ānwèi.
text_en: Every evening around six, the sound of stir-frying rises from downstairs. First the crackling of hot oil, then the sizzle of ingredients hitting the wok, then the rhythm of a spatula turning. If you listen carefully, you can even smell the cooking fumes drifting up through the window. I don't know which household is cooking, but I'm more familiar with their menu than with my neighbors' names — from the sounds I can tell whether today is stir-fry or stew, simple or elaborate. Weekend sounds are usually richer, lasting longer, as if someone is making a proper meal. Weekdays are quick, done in fifteen minutes. Sometimes when I come home from work exhausted, wanting to do nothing, hearing the cooking start downstairs makes me feel the day still has somewhere to land. It's not my dinner, but the sound itself is a kind of comfort.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎么判断楼下做的是什么菜？
questions.q_en: How does the author tell what's being cooked downstairs?

questions.q_zh: 周末和平时的声音有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How do weekend and weekday sounds differ?

options.pinyin: zhōumò gèng fēngfù, chíxù gèng cháng
options.text_en: Weekends are richer and last longer

questions.q_zh: 听到炒菜声给作者什么感觉？
questions.q_en: How does hearing the cooking make the author feel?

options.text: 安慰，觉得这一天还有着落
options.pinyin: ānwèi, juéde zhè yì tiān háiyǒu zheluò
options.text_en: Comforted, feeling the day still has somewhere to land

title: The Convenience Store at 3 AM
text_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店是一个没有身份的空间。白天你走进去是顾客、员工、学生、上班族，但凌晨三点，你只是一个需要东西的人。灯光很亮，跟外面的黑暗形成一种奇怪的对比。货架上的商品整整齐齐地排列着，不管外面的世界多混乱，这里永远井然有序。收银台后面的店员通常在看手机或者打瞌睡，你们之间的交流缩减到最少：扫码、付款、一个点头。来便利店的人各有各的原因：失眠的、加班的、刚从酒吧出来的、找不到回家路的。每个人进来的时候带着各自的故事，出去的时候手里多了一瓶水或者一个饭团。便利店不问你为什么来，它只是在那里，亮着灯等你。
text_pinyin: Língchén sān diǎn de biànlì diàn shì yí gè méiyǒu shēnfèn de kōngjiān. Báitiān nǐ zǒu jìnqù shì gùkè, yuángōng, xuéshēng, shàngbānzú, dàn língchén sān diǎn, nǐ zhǐ shì yí gè xūyào dōngxi de rén. Dēngguāng hěn liàng, gēn wàimiàn de hēi'àn xíngchéng yì zhǒng qíguài de duìbǐ. Huòjià shàng de shāngpǐn zhěngzhěngqíqí de páiliè zhe, bùguǎn wàimiàn de shìjiè duō hùnluàn, zhèlǐ yǒngyuǎn jǐngrán yǒuxù. Shōuyín tái hòumiàn de diànyuán tōngcháng zài kàn shǒujī huòzhě dǎ kēshuì, nǐmen zhī jiān de jiāoliú suōjiǎn dào zuì shǎo: sǎo mǎ, fùkuǎn, yí gè diǎntóu. Lái biànlì diàn de rén gè yǒu gè de yuányīn: shīmián de, jiābān de, gāng cóng jiǔbā chūlái de, zhǎo bú dào huíjiā lù de. Měi gè rén jìnlái de shíhou dài zhe gèzì de gùshi, chūqù de shíhou shǒu lǐ duō le yì píng shuǐ huòzhě yí gè fàntuán. Biànlì diàn bú wèn nǐ wèishénme lái, tā zhǐ shì zài nàlǐ, liàng zhe dēng děng nǐ.
text_en: A convenience store at 3 AM is an identity-free space. During the day you walk in as a customer, employee, student, office worker — but at 3 AM, you're just a person who needs something. The lights are bright, forming a strange contrast with the darkness outside. Products line the shelves in perfect order; no matter how chaotic the world outside, this place is always orderly. The cashier behind the counter is usually on their phone or dozing; the exchange between you is reduced to a minimum: scan, pay, a nod. People come to the convenience store for different reasons: insomnia, overtime, just left a bar, can't find the way home. Each person enters carrying their own story and leaves with a bottle of water or a rice ball in hand. The convenience store doesn't ask why you're here. It just sits there, lights on, waiting for you.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说凌晨的便利店是「没有身份的空间」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author call the late-night convenience store an 'identity-free space'?

lǐmiàn rén duō, wàimiàn rén shǎo

title: The Person I Am on Vacation
text_zh: 每次出去旅行，大概到第三天，我会变成一个完全不同的人。头两天还带着工作的惯性——走路快、吃饭快、时不时看手机。但到了第三天，某个时刻，身体里好像有一个开关被关掉了。我开始走得很慢，开始注意路边的花，开始在咖啡馆坐很久而不觉得是浪费时间。那个时候我会想：这才是真的我，还是这只是一个暂时的版本？每次旅行结束回到日常生活，这个「度假版的我」会在大约一天半之内消失。邮件、会议、截止日期像水一样漫上来，把那个缓慢的人淹没。也许问题不是「哪个才是真的我」，而是「为什么我的日常生活不允许那个人存在」。
text_pinyin: Měi cì chūqù lǚxíng, dàgài dào dì sān tiān, wǒ huì biàn chéng yí gè wánquán bùtóng de rén. Tóu liǎng tiān hái dài zhe gōngzuò de guànxìng — zǒulù kuài, chīfàn kuài, shí bù shí kàn shǒujī. Dàn dào le dì sān tiān, mǒu gè shíkè, shēntǐ lǐ hǎoxiàng yǒu yí gè kāiguān bèi guāndiào le. Wǒ kāishǐ zǒu de hěn màn, kāishǐ zhùyì lù biān de huā, kāishǐ zài kāfēiguǎn zuò hěn jiǔ ér bù juéde shì làngfèi shíjiān. Nàge shíhou wǒ huì xiǎng: zhè cái shì zhēn de wǒ, háishì zhè zhǐ shì yí gè zànshí de bǎnběn? Měi cì lǚxíng jiéshù huí dào rìcháng shēnghuó, zhège「dùjià bǎn de wǒ」huì zài dàyuē yì tiān bàn zhī nèi xiāoshī. Yóujiàn, huìyì, jiézhǐ rìqī xiàng shuǐ yíyàng màn shànglái, bǎ nàge huǎnmàn de rén yānmò. Yěxǔ wèntí bú shì「nǎge cái shì zhēn de wǒ」, ér shì「wèishénme wǒ de rìcháng shēnghuó bù yǔnxǔ nàge rén cúnzài」.
text_en: Every time I travel, around the third day, I become a completely different person. The first two days I still carry work's momentum — walking fast, eating fast, checking my phone. But on the third day, at some moment, it's as if a switch inside me gets flipped off. I start walking slowly, noticing flowers by the road, sitting in cafés for a long time without feeling it's a waste. In those moments I wonder: is this the real me, or just a temporary version? Every time I return from a trip to daily life, this 'vacation me' disappears within about a day and a half. Emails, meetings, deadlines rise like water, drowning the slow person. Perhaps the question isn't 'which is the real me,' but 'why doesn't my daily life allow that person to exist.'

questions.q_en: Around which day of travel does the author change?

questions.q_zh: 回到日常生活后，「度假版的我」多久消失？

options.text_en: About a day and a half

questions.q_en: What is the author's ultimate question?

options.text: 为什么日常生活不允许那个人存在

options.text_en: Why daily life doesn't allow that person to exist

options.text: 度假版的自己是不是假的

options.text_en: Whether the vacation self is fake

title: The Library's Quietest Floor
text_zh: 大学图书馆的每一层都有不同的声音水平。一楼最吵，因为有咖啡区和小组讨论室。二楼稍微安静一些，大部分人在看书。三楼基本不允许说话，只有翻页和打字的声音。但四楼是真正的安静层——连打字声都被认为太吵了。四楼的人都有一种默契：你可以翻书，但要轻；你可以喝水，但瓶子不能有声音；你的手机必须静音，如果震动被听到，你会收到来自四面八方的目光。我在四楼学习了整整一个学期，离开以后发现自己养成了一个习惯：在任何安静的空间里，我会自动把所有动作的音量降到最低。四楼教会了我一种对安静的尊重。
text_pinyin: Dàxué túshūguǎn de měi yí céng dōu yǒu bùtóng de shēngyīn shuǐpíng. Yī lóu zuì chǎo, yīnwèi yǒu kāfēi qū hé xiǎozǔ tǎolùn shì. Èr lóu shāowēi ānjìng yìxiē, dà bùfèn rén zài kàn shū. Sān lóu jīběn bù yǔnxǔ shuōhuà, zhǐ yǒu fānyè hé dǎzì de shēngyīn. Dàn sì lóu shì zhēnzhèng de ānjìng céng — lián dǎzì shēng dōu bèi rènwéi tài chǎo le. Sì lóu de rén dōu yǒu yì zhǒng mòqì: nǐ kěyǐ fān shū, dàn yào qīng; nǐ kěyǐ hē shuǐ, dàn píngzi bù néng yǒu shēngyīn; nǐ de shǒujī bìxū jìngyīn, rúguǒ zhèndòng bèi tīng dào, nǐ huì shōudào lái zì sì miàn bā fāng de mùguāng. Wǒ zài sì lóu xuéxí le zhěngzhěng yí gè xuéqī, líkāi yǐhòu fāxiàn zìjǐ yǎngchéng le yí gè xíguàn: zài rènhé ānjìng de kōngjiān lǐ, wǒ huì zìdòng bǎ suǒyǒu dòngzuò de yīnliàng jiàng dào zuì dī. Sì lóu jiāo huì le wǒ yì zhǒng duì ānjìng de zūnzhòng.
text_en: Each floor of the university library has a different sound level. The first floor is loudest, with a coffee area and group discussion rooms. The second floor is slightly quieter, most people reading. The third floor basically doesn't allow talking — just the sound of pages turning and typing. But the fourth floor is the true quiet floor — even typing is considered too loud. Fourth-floor people share an understanding: you can turn pages, but gently; you can drink water, but the bottle can't make noise; your phone must be silent, and if vibration is heard, you'll receive looks from all directions. I studied on the fourth floor for an entire semester. After leaving, I discovered I'd developed a habit: in any quiet space, I automatically lower the volume of every action to minimum. The fourth floor taught me a kind of respect for silence.

questions.q_zh: 四楼跟三楼有什么区别？
questions.q_en: How does the fourth floor differ from the third?

options.text: 三楼可以打字，四楼连打字都太吵
options.pinyin: sān lóu kěyǐ dǎzì, sì lóu lián dǎzì dōu tài chǎo
options.text_en: Third floor allows typing; fourth floor considers even typing too loud

options.pinyin: sì lóu de rén gèng shǎo
options.text_en: Fewer people on the fourth floor

questions.q_zh: 手机震动会怎样？
questions.q_en: What happens if a phone vibrates?

options.text: 收到来自四面八方的目光
options.pinyin: shōudào lái zì sì miàn bā fāng de mùguāng

questions.q_zh: 四楼教会了作者什么？
questions.q_en: What did the fourth floor teach the author?

options.text_en: How to get along with strangers

title: The Note Under the Windshield
text_zh: 今天早上发现车的挡风玻璃下面夹着一张纸条，上面用圆珠笔写着：「你的车灯昨晚亮了一整夜。我帮你关了。——三楼的邻居。」我完全不知道是谁。这栋楼有八十多户，我只认识两三家。但某个我不认识的人，在昨天深夜注意到我的车灯没关，然后下楼帮我关掉了——虽然帮别人关车灯需要知道怎么开车门，这意味着他先试了一下，发现门没锁。这就引出了第二个问题：我昨晚忘了锁车门。一个善意的行为暴露了另一个疏忽。我把纸条夹在了后视镜上，决定今天烤一盘饼干放在三楼的走廊上，附一张纸条：「谢谢。——五楼忘锁车门的人。」
text_pinyin: Jīntiān zǎoshang fāxiàn chē de dǎngfēng bōli xiàmiàn jiā zhe yì zhāng zhǐtiáo, shàngmiàn yòng yuánzhūbǐ xiě zhe:「Nǐ de chēdēng zuówǎn liàng le yì zhěng yè. Wǒ bāng nǐ guān le. — sān lóu de línjū.」Wǒ wánquán bù zhīdào shì shéi. Zhè dòng lóu yǒu bāshí duō hù, wǒ zhǐ rènshi liǎng sān jiā. Dàn mǒu gè wǒ bú rènshi de rén, zài zuótiān shēnyè zhùyì dào wǒ de chēdēng méi guān, ránhòu xià lóu bāng wǒ guān diào le — suīrán bāng biérén guān chēdēng xūyào zhīdào zěnme kāi chēmén, zhè yìwèi zhe tā xiān shì le yíxià, fāxiàn mén méi suǒ. Zhè jiù yǐn chū le dì èr gè wèntí: wǒ zuówǎn wàng le suǒ chēmén. Yí gè shànyì de xíngwéi bàolù le lìng yí gè shūhu. Wǒ bǎ zhǐtiáo jiā zài le hòushì jìng shàng, juédìng jīntiān kǎo yì pán bǐnggān fàng zài sān lóu de zǒuláng shàng, fù yì zhāng zhǐtiáo:「Xièxie. — wǔ lóu wàng suǒ chēmén de rén.」
text_en: This morning I found a note tucked under my car's windshield, written in ballpoint pen: 'Your headlights were on all night. I turned them off for you. — Your third-floor neighbor.' I have no idea who this is. This building has over eighty units; I only know two or three. But someone I don't know noticed my headlights were on late last night, then went downstairs and turned them off — though turning off someone's headlights requires opening the car door, which means they tried first and found it unlocked. This raises a second issue: I forgot to lock my car last night. One act of kindness exposed another negligence. I clipped the note to my rearview mirror and decided to bake a tray of cookies today, leave them in the third-floor hallway with a note: 'Thank you. — The person on the fifth floor who forgot to lock their car.'
